verdicts.symbol: apply_V / apply_V_ring / bse_simple V-term / bse_serial V-term (exchange kernel)
verdicts.reasoning: Read all four implementations (bse_serial.py:62-64, bse_jax.py apply_V:108-121, bse_ring_comm.py apply_V_ring:276-300, bse_simple.py:86-131). All four compute S/U with einsum output subscripts that retain 'k' (e.g. 'kcvN,bcvk->bNk'), and the only collectives (lax.psum/psum_scatter over mesh axes 'x'/'y') shard bands (c/v via make_bse_shardings: X=P(None,'x','y',None), k is the un-sharded trailing axis) — k is never contracted anywhere in the V-term. Re-derived the physics from BerkeleyGW's own kernel_main.f90: the outer loop is 'do ik=1,qg%nf' with mtxel_kernel called per (ik,ikp) pair for EVERY ik×ikp combination (confirmed lines 300, 407-410, 472-478) — i.e. BGW's bsex is dense across k,k', not block-diagonal. The correct K^x application is (K^x X)_{cvk} = (1/Nk)Σ_μ M*_cvk(μ)Σ_ν V0(μν)[Σ_{c'v'k'} M_c'v'k'(ν)X_{c'v'k'}] — the bracket must NOT depend on k. LORRAX's code produces a per-k S(ν,k) instead, i.e. computes only the k'=k terms of K^x, silently dropping ALL cross-k exchange coupling (responsible for LO-TO/singlet-triplet splitting in real BSE). The module's own docstring calling this 'q=0 exchange, rank-1 in centroid basis' is consistent with my derivation (v(μν) truly has no q-dependence) — which is exactly why it SHOULD be summed over all k, not restricted to k. STATUS.md's 3 meV Si validation is plausibly insensitive per the claim's own caveat (weak exchange element / triplet-dominated lowest manifold), so it does not contradict this finding. This is a serious, live, reproducible physics defect confirmed independently via BGW source cross-check, present identically in all 4 matvec implementations.

Read bse_ring_comm.py:183 and the gather-mode duplicate at ~551: both use einsum string 'kvsM,bvksN->bMNtsk'. Reproduced directly: `numpy.einsum('kvsM,bvksN->bMNtsk', a, b)` raises `ValueError: einstein sum subscripts string included output subscript 't' which never appeared in an input` — verified this crashes at call time, not just semantically wrong. Compared against the working sibling `_ring_sum_conduction` (line ~119: 'kctM,bcksN->bMNtsk') where psi_c_slice legitimately carries a 't' (conduction spinor axis) — confirming the valence-side function forgot to give psi_v_slice a distinct spinor letter (used 's' which collides with the conduction-spinor axis already baked into `buf`), exactly matching the reader's proposed fix 'kvtM,...'. Traced callers: `_encode_T_B` (calls `_ring_sum_valence_second`) and `_encode_T_B_gather` (inlines the same broken string) are both used by `_apply_B` inside `build_bse_ring_matvec_full`, reached by ALL of bse_feast.py:494, bse_kpm.py:131, bse_w_exact.py:106, bse_pseudopoles.py:242 whenever non-TDA + include_W=True. Confirmed via argparse help text (bse_pseudopoles.py:569: '--tda... (default full non-TDA)') and bse_w_exact.py args (--tda/--rpa both default False ⇒ use_tda=False, include_W=True) that non-TDA + W is the DEFAULT for every one of these CLI entry points. This is an unconditional crash on the documented default of 4 separate module CLIs.

Read bse_io.py:221-260: `_read_vq0_sharded` unconditionally does `n_rmu = dset.shape[6]` and later `dset[0,0,0,0,0,0,mu_start:mu_end,nu_start:nu_end]` (8 explicit indices) with NO ndim branch/shim, unlike its sibling `_read_wq_sharded` which does have a 3-layout shim (8-D/6-D/3-D, bse_io.py:285-317). Confirmed the modern writer produces rank-3 flat-q V_qmunu: gw_output.py:202-208 comment states V_qmunu/W0_qmunu are rank-3 flat-q, and `write_restart_state_to_h5` (file_io/tagged_arrays.py `_write('V_qmunu', V_qmunu, mu_axes=(-2,-1))`) preserves whatever rank the caller passes (rank-3 in the modern pipeline). Confirmed the actual call site `V_q0 = _read_vq0_sharded(vq_dset, ...)` at bse_io.py:460 passes the raw on-disk dataset directly with no reshape/shim applied beforehand. For a rank-3 dataset, `dset.shape[6]` raises IndexError immediately — reproduced the equivalent numpy behavior. This reader was never updated when the kgrid header shim was added to `_read_wq_sharded` (confirmed by direct comparison of the two functions), exactly as claimed.

verdicts.symbol: _read_wq_sharded flat-q kgrid contract (restart format drift, part 2)
verdicts.reasoning: Read bse_io.py:296-317: the 3-D flat-q branch requires `'kgrid' in dset.attrs` (an HDF5 attribute ON the dataset object) and raises ValueError with the exact message quoted in the claim if absent. Read the writer side: `write_restart_state_to_h5` (tagged_arrays.py:81-82) calls `io.write_attr('kgrid', ...)`, and `write_attr` in `_slab_io_allgather.py:120-137` does `self._file.create_dataset(name, data=host)` — writes kgrid as a TOP-LEVEL HDF5 DATASET (`f['kgrid']`), never as `dset.attrs['kgrid']` on V_qmunu/W0_qmunu. Confirmed the caller `load_bse_data_from_restart_sharded` (bse_io.py:398-401) independently resolves kgrid correctly via `elif 'kgrid' in f:` (top-level dataset) for its OWN n_rmu/kgrid bookkeeping, but then calls `_read_wq_sharded(wq_dset, mu_per_x, nu_per_y, mesh_xy, n_rmu_pad, trim=False)` at bse_io.py:461 — no kgrid argument in the function signature at all, so the resolved kgrid is never threaded through. On any modern flat-q restart, `_read_wq_sharded` hits its own `dset.attrs['kgrid']` check, finds it absent, and raises ValueError at :312-317. Confirmed as a real follow-up bug once bug #2 is fixed, exactly as claimed.

verdicts.symbol: _load_ring_subset q=0 head injection ordering (restart format drift, part 3)
verdicts.reasoning: Re-derived the indexing math: head_correction.py:773 does `V_qmunu.at[..., 0, 0, 0, :, :].add(...)`, which requires the array's last 5 axes to be (nkx,nky,nkz,mu,nu) — true for 8-D legacy, coincidentally true for 6-D (npol=1 so the extra two 0-indices are harmless), but for 3-D flat-q `(nq,mu,nu)` this indexes 5 concrete positions into a 3-axis array → IndexError ('too many indices'). Confirmed the call at bse_io.py:828 (`apply_q0_head_rank1(V_qmunu, W0_qmunu, ...)`) runs BEFORE the ndim shim at :846 (`if V_qmunu.ndim == 8:` ... else flat-q branch at :856-872) — i.e. on the raw, unnormalized on-disk array. Confirmed the current writer always produces the trigger condition: G0_mu_nu is written unconditionally alongside V_qmunu (gw_init.py:664, tagged_arrays.py:94), and vhead is populated from `head_static.vc0` in gw_output.py:231 in the standard restart-write path (not behind an opt-in flag), so `G0_mu_nu is not None and (vhead is not None or whead is not None)` at bse_io.py:817 is true for ordinary restarts. Single-device BSE (`_load_ring_subset`, called by e.g. bse_ring_comm.py, bse_jax.py's non-sharded branch) on any current-format restart with head data crashes at :828.

Read bse_io.py:139-146: `_pad_axis_to_multiple` returns `(x, size)` on the pad==0 early-return AND `(jnp.pad(x, pad_width, ...), size)` on the padded branch — `size` is captured as `x.shape[axis]` BEFORE padding in both cases, so the second return element is always the PRE-pad extent, never the actual padded extent. Confirmed both call sites (bse_io.py ~450-451 for the sharded loader, ~899-900/915-917 for `_load_ring_subset`) bind this to `n_val_pad`/`n_cond_pad` and store them in the returned data dict. Confirmed the consumer `bse_lanczos.py:140-146` reads `nc_pad = int(data['n_cond_pad'])`, `nv_pad = int(data['n_val_pad'])` and builds trial-vector `shape = (bs, nc_pad, nv_pad, nk)` from these values, which will be smaller than the psi tensors' actual padded band axis whenever `n_cond % grid_x != 0` or `n_val % grid_y != 0` (since only in that case does pad != 0 and the discrepancy between 'size' and true padded extent appear). This is a real, reproducible naming/semantics bug — silently benign at pad==0 (i.e. whenever band counts are divisible by the mesh, which matches all validated runs cited: 4/8 val/cond on 2×2), but would break/crash on non-divisible band counts, matching the claim's characterization exactly.

verdicts.symbol: _preview_lanczos / run_haydock eqp branch uses unclamped CLI band counts
verdicts.reasoning: Read bse_jax.py:238-258 and absorption_haydock.py:166-186: both compute `val_idx = np.arange(n_occ_eff - n_val, n_occ_eff)` using the raw CLI-derived `n_val`/`n_cond` closures, then index `enk_full_np[:, val_idx]`. Confirmed the loader `load_bse_data_from_restart_sharded` (bse_io.py:426-427) clamps its OWN local `n_val`/`n_cond` parameters (`n_val = min(n_val, n_val_available)`) but this is a local Python rebind inside that function's scope — it does not propagate back to the caller's `n_val` variable (int, immutable, passed by value). If the user's requested `--n-val`/`--n-cond` exceeds available bands, `n_occ_eff - n_val` can go negative, and `enk_full_np[:, val_idx]` with negative fancy indices silently wraps to the tail of the array (numpy/jax fancy-indexing semantics) rather than erroring — silently selecting wrong (high-lying) bands instead of valence bands. Confirmed the contrast case: `davidson_absorption.py:93-96` retrieves `n_val_eff = int(data['n_val'])` from the RETURNED (clamped) data dict rather than re-using the raw CLI variable — the 'one careful copy' the claim describes. This is a real, reproducible silent-wrong-answer bug on the (admittedly edge-case) path where a user over-requests bands.

Enumerated all call mechanisms: grep -rn 'apply_bse_hamiltonian\b' across src/, tests/, tools/, scripts/, docs/ (repo-relative to sources/lorrax_D) → only 3 hits: bse_jax.py:46 (__all__ list), bse_jax.py:68 (def), and src/bse/context/README.md:130 (design-doc mention, not executable code). Grepped runs/**/*.sh and skills/**/*.md for both `apply_bse_hamiltonian` and `bse_jax.apply_V`/`apply_W` module-level symbols — zero hits (the only bse_jax invocations found in runs/ are `python -m bse.bse_jax` CLI calls, which route through `__main__` → `_preview_lanczos`, confirmed NOT calling `apply_bse_hamiltonian`). Confirmed bse_jax.py's own `apply_D`/`apply_V`/`apply_W` (module-level, distinct from bse_serial's copies) are used ONLY inside `apply_bse_hamiltonian` (lines 83-85) and nowhere else in the file. Additionally verified the reader's bonus claim that it's uncallable-as-exported: `apply_bse_hamiltonian` is decorated with plain `@jax.jit` (bse_jax.py:67) yet `apply_V` internally calls `lax.psum(S_partial, axis_name='x')` — this requires an active named mesh axis from `shard_map`/`pmap`, absent under plain `jax.jit`, so even a hypothetical caller would hit a JAX axis-name-not-bound error. Confirmed dead by exhaustive grep and confirmed broken by construction.

Grepped each symbol across src/, tests/, tools/, scripts/, docs/, runs/**/*.sh, skills/**/*.md. `symmetrize_W_q`: only bse_jax.py:31 (import), bse_jax.py:63 (__all__), bse_serial.py:12 (def) — zero call sites. `apply_bse_hamiltonian_ring`: only bse_jax.py:19 (import), bse_jax.py:47 (__all__), bse_ring_comm.py:303 (def) — zero call sites; confirmed the actual matvecs used by build_bse_ring_matvec/_full are locally-defined closures (`_matvec_impl`/`_apply_A`/`_apply_B`) that do NOT call the top-level `apply_bse_hamiltonian_ring` function. `BSEData`: only bse_io.py:18 (class def, `class BSEData(SimpleNamespace): pass`) — never instantiated anywhere; all data flow in the module uses plain dicts. No `python -m`, getattr/string-dispatch, or argparse subcommand references to any of the three found anywhere in the searched trees. Confirmed dead, and the hermiticity concern the claim raises (H relies entirely on GW-side W symmetry with no in-package enforcement) is a valid, verifiable consequence of the dead status.

Read src/bse/test_bse.py:42-149: confirmed it reads `psi_l`/`psi_r`/`enk_l`/`enk_r` (bse_io.py:71-76) and assumes 8-D V_qmunu (`V_qmunu.shape[3:6]` for kgrid at line 85, `V_qmunu[0,0,0,0,0,0,:,:]` for q=0 at line ~143) — none of which match the canonical modern writer's `psi_full_y`/`enk_full` + flat-q (nq,μ,ν) V_qmunu schema (confirmed via tagged_arrays.py's `_write` dataset list: V_qmunu/S_qmunu/V0_noG0_munu/G0_mu_nu/psi_full_y/enk_full). Additionally confirmed via `pyproject.toml:59-60` (`[tool.pytest.ini_options] testpaths = ['tests']`) that pytest only collects from the top-level `tests/` directory — `src/bse/test_bse.py` is never actually run by `pytest -q` despite functions named `test_matvec`/`test_pair_amplitude`/`test_lanczos` (which would themselves fail pytest fixture resolution if ever collected, since they take a positional `data` arg with no fixture). This is a manually-invoked CLI script (`python -m bse.test_bse`) with genuinely stale/broken internal data-loading logic — confirmed cruft, not silently wrong in the automated suite since it isn't part of it.

verdicts.symbol: bse_jax CLI default routing + 1-device knob drop + delegation flag loss
verdicts.reasoning: Verified (a): bse_jax.py:520/542 `use_rpa = args.rpa or not args.bse` — with default `--bse` unset (False), use_rpa=True regardless of --rpa, confirming RPA (D+V only, no W) is the silent default. Verified (b): bse_jax.py's 1-device call `solve_bse(psi_c, psi_v, eps_c, eps_v, W_q, V_q0, nkx, nky, nkz, n_eig=n_eig, max_iter=max_lanczos_iter, include_W=include_W)` (line ~325) passes no `n_reorth`, so `solve_bse`'s default `n_reorth: int = 10` (bse_lanczos.py:45) silently applies, contradicting STATUS.md:15's explicit guidance that `--n-reorth -1` (full reorth) is 'the right default for spinor BSE'; separately confirmed `absorption_haydock.py:158-161` hard-raises `RuntimeError` below 2 devices. Verified (c): read the full kpm_argv (bse_jax.py:516-536) and feast argv (bse_jax.py:543-604) blocks — neither contains `--n-occ` or `--eqp`; confirmed via `grep '"--n-occ"\|"--eqp"' bse_feast.py bse_kpm.py bse_jax.py` that ONLY bse_jax.py defines these flags at all — bse_feast.py/bse_kpm.py have no such argparse options, so QP corrections and explicit n_occ are structurally unavailable on the default (non-`--lanczos`) FEAST/KPM route, not merely 'unforwarded'. This is a genuine, confirmed functional gap (silent flag loss / wrong-defaults-by-default), not a documented convention — I reclassify from 'convention' to a confirmed defect in default behavior.

verdicts.symbol: spin handling: charge-channel W, no singlet factor, BGW-compat flips at I/O only
verdicts.reasoning: Verified bse_serial.py:29 `compute_pair_amplitude`: `jnp.einsum('kcsm,kvsm->kcvm', ...)` traces spinor index s, confirmed. Verified bse_serial.py:69-70: `R = einsum('kvsN,bcvk->bcksN', ...)`; `T = einsum('kctM,bcksN->bMNtsk', ...)` — t (conduction spinor) and s (valence spinor) kept as separate axes, and `U_R = W_R[None,:,:,None,None,...] * T_R` broadcasts W with no spinor axes at all — confirms W is charge-channel-only, consistent with the independently-documented bispinor screened-W roadmap. Verified bse_io.py write_eigenvectors_stream: RYD2EV conversion present (~line 40) and `vec = vec[:, :, ::-1]` valence-axis flip with an explicit comment citing `BSE/input_fi.f90:407`; cross-checked against src/bse/STATUS.md's 'Index ordering' section which independently documents the same valence-flip/eV convention sourced from the same BGW file. All cited evidence checks out exactly, and the claim itself is appropriately hedged (explicitly flags the nspinor=1 caveat as an open risk rather than asserting a proven bug). Accurately characterized as BGW-compat convention, not a bug.

Confirmed `compute_pair_amplitude`/`_pair_amplitude` defined independently 3x via grep (bse_preconditioner.py:44, bse_jax.py:94, bse_serial.py:27). Confirmed `_encode_T`/`encode_T_ring` (bse_ring_comm.py, inside build_bse_ring_matvec) and `_apply_W_from_T`/`apply_W_from_T` are BYTE-IDENTICAL to their counterparts inside build_bse_ring_matvec_full — read both copies side by side and they are character-for-character the same function bodies duplicated verbatim, exactly as the claim states. Combined with the independently-confirmed eqp-override triplication (claim 7) and the dead module-level matvec quartet (claim 8) being a third parallel copy of the D/V/W kernel math, this is a well-evidenced, accurate refactor finding consistent with the project's documented no-redundancy standard.

verdicts.symbol: correlated random probes (same PRNGKey for Re and Im)
verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed bse_io.py:914-917 exactly as cited: `key = jax.random.PRNGKey(0); X = jax.random.normal(key, shape) + 1j*jax.random.normal(key, shape)` — identical key for both real and imaginary draws (real part == imag part). Confirmed bse_ring_comm.py:867-874 has the same pattern, actually more pervasive there — the smoke test reuses the single `key` across psi_c, psi_v, eps_c, eps_v, W_q, AND X, not just Re/Im of one array. This is real, reproducible, low-severity: harmless for the norm-level correctness checks currently in use (as the claim itself concedes) but does reduce the diagnostic power of `ring_matvec_correctness_check`/smoke tests against phase-sensitive bugs (notably, it would not have caught claim 1's exchange-term issue since that's magnitude-visible, but could mask other phase-dependent errors). Accurately characterized as benign cruft.

claims.detail: Both CLIs unconditionally pass use_nohead=args.nohead to load_bse_data_from_restart_sharded, whose signature has no such parameter → TypeError('unexpected keyword argument use_nohead') on EVERY invocation, with or without --nohead. Also kills the bse_jax --kpm-dos route (bse_jax.py:536 delegates to bse_kpm.main). The FEAST --windows-kpm path is unaffected (calls run_kpm_dos directly on pre-loaded data). History: a0da0a5 deleted these files as 'broken on main (import error)'; fe5e3e8 restored them without reconciling with the rewritten loader.

claims.detail: Help promises headless arrays 'V_qmunu_nohead, W0_qmunu_nohead' for head-on/off A/B debugging; no code at HEAD reads datasets by those names and the loader lost its use_nohead parameter. Parsed-but-unread ⇒ LOST WIRING for a head-A/B knob, not dead config — the head-injection machinery it toggled (apply_q0_head_rank1_sharded) still exists.
claims.evidence: grep -rn 'nohead' src/ tests/ tools/ → only the two argparse sites + the two crashing call sites; bse_io.py has no *_nohead dataset access (grep 'V_qmunu' shows only f['V_qmunu'] at bse_io.py:375).

claims.detail: Flag is parsed but args.ring_timing is referenced nowhere in the file or repo; its sole consumer ring_matvec_timing was deleted in cleanup commit a0da0a5 (which also removed the companion --repeat/--warmup flags but missed this one). Checked all call mechanisms: no getattr/string dispatch on 'ring_timing' anywhere.
claims.evidence: bse_jax.py:481: parser.add_argument("--ring-timing", action="store_true"); regex scan of argparse dests vs args.<dest> reports --ring-timing as the only unreferenced dest in bse_jax; git show a0da0a5: 'bse_jax.py: removed unreachable ring_matvec_timing(...) call … plus the --repeat/--warmup CLI args that only fed it'.

claims.detail: Parsed and shown in STATUS.md's recommended --lanczos command (STATUS.md:105 '--px 2 --py 2'), but the Lanczos path builds its mesh with create_mesh_2d() auto-factorization of ALL devices and the single-device fallback hardcodes 1,1 — the values are silently ignored. Only the feast/kpm/ring-check delegation routes forward them.
claims.evidence: bse_jax.py:229: mesh_xy = create_mesh_2d() (no args); bse_jax.py:295-300: _load_ring_subset(restart_file, n_val, n_cond, 1, 1, …); bse_ring_comm.py:31-43: create_mesh_2d factorizes len(jax.devices()); _preview_lanczos signature (bse_jax.py:203-219) has no px/py parameter.

claims.symbol: --eqp/--n-occ silently dropped on bse_jax FEAST and KPM routes
claims.detail: bse_jax accepts --eqp/--n-occ globally, but the delegation argv lists for bse_kpm.main and bse_feast.main omit them and neither sub-CLI defines such flags — a user running the DEFAULT route (no --lanczos) with --eqp gets FEAST windows/Ritz values computed from uncorrected DFT energies with no warning. Concrete wrong output: Si 8×8 with eqp shifts of ~0.7 eV → all FEAST window classifications and reported eigenvalues remain at DFT positions while the user believes QP corrections were applied.
claims.evidence: bse_jax.py:543-602 feast argv contains only -i/--n-val/--n-cond/--px/--py/--n-lanczos/--buffer/--n-quad1/--n-quad2/--quadrature/--units-ev-per-ry/--feast-ritz*/--gmres-*/--rpa/--tda/--windows-kpm*/--kpm-*/--window1/2; grep add_argument src/bse/bse_feast.py → no --eqp, no --n-occ; args.eqp read only at bse_jax.py:617 inside the --lanczos branch.

claims.symbol: default kernel is RPA, not BSE
claims.detail: use_rpa = args.rpa or not args.bse and include_W = not (args.rpa or not args.bse): omitting --bse silently drops the −W direct term on every bse_jax route. Documented in STATUS.md's recommended command (always passes --bse) but a plain invocation quietly computes RPA. Footgun, not a bug — behavior matches the (subtle) help text.
claims.evidence: bse_jax.py:515: use_rpa = args.rpa or not args.bse; :616: include_W=not (args.rpa or not args.bse); --bse help (382-383): 'Use full BSE kernel (D+V-W). Overrides default RPA.'

claims.detail: args, _ = parser.parse_known_args(): any misspelled option (e.g. --n-reorth=-1 typo'd as --nreorth) is silently discarded instead of erroring — dangerous combined with the RPA-default and TDA-required gotchas. Every other BSE CLI uses strict parse_args.
claims.evidence: bse_jax.py:484: args, _ = parser.parse_known_args() (unique in src/bse; all other mains call parser.parse_args(argv)).

claims.detail: STATUS.md module table says bse_simple is 'fastest, default --matvec-kind=simple' but the argparse default is 'ring'. Doc/code mismatch — one of the two should change.
claims.evidence: bse_jax.py:446-449: parser.add_argument("--matvec-kind", choices=("ring","gather","simple"), default="ring"); STATUS.md:16: 'bse_simple.py | plain-jit (μ,ν) matvec … | fastest, default --matvec-kind=simple'.

claims.detail: Haydock defaults n_spinor=2 (spinor) and feeds the ε₂ prefactor 16π²/(V·Nk·n_spin·n_spinor); the eigenvector route instead infers it from eigenvectors.h5 spin_kernel. A scalar-relativistic haydock run without --n-spinor 1 gets ε₂ halved. Per-element math: eps2 ∝ 1/n_spinor, so nspinor=2 on an nspinor=1 system → exact factor-2 under-report.
claims.evidence: absorption_haydock.py:317: p.add_argument("--n-spinor", type=int, default=2, …); absorption_eigvecs.py:124: n_spinor = args.n_spinor if args.n_spinor is not None else params["nspinor"]; prefactor absorption_eigvecs.py:81: pref = 16.0*np.pi**2/(V_cell*n_k*n_spin*n_spinor).

Any other cohsex.in key is silently ignored by BSE with no unknown-key warning. Violates the single-source-of-truth convention (no-redundancy rule); consolidation candidate for the refactor map.
claims.evidence: grep -n 'gw_config\|read_lorrax_input' src/bse/*.py → no hits; bse_io.py:594-596: key,_,val = stripped.partition("="); if key.strip()=="wfn_file": …; gw_config.py:348-349: "vhead": None, "whead_0freq": None.

claims.evidence: test_bse.py:337: restart_file = find_restart_file(args.input) (local def, module has private candidates block ending test_bse.py:302-306); bse_io.py:756: def _find_restart_file(input_file).

claims.symbol: two Ry→eV constants in the BSE tree
claims.detail: pseudopoles_eval falls back to ry_to_ev=13.605693122994 (CODATA-style) when the poles H5 lacks the attr, while every other module uses 13.6056980659. 4×10⁻⁶ eV/Ry discrepancy — negligible numerically but a consistency smell for the flag audit.
claims.evidence: pseudopoles_eval.py:144: ry_to_ev = float(h5.attrs.get("ry_to_ev", 13.605693122994)); bse_feast.py:29: RY_TO_EV_DEFAULT = 13.6056980659; bse_kpm.py:34, pseudopoles_sweep.py:23, absorption_common.py:23 same 13.6056980659.

claims.detail: Test-only μ-pad-extent knob (device-count-invariance testing at fixed P) that silently affects BSE array shapes if exported; padding.py explicitly forbids production use. Listed so the flag catalog notes BSE inherits it through padded_mu_extent.
claims.evidence: padding.py:37: 'TEST-ONLY: extra μ-pad rows requested via LORRAX_EXTRA_MU_PAD'; :54: 'NEVER set this in production runs'; bse_io.py:442: n_rmu_pad = padded_mu_extent(n_rmu, grid_x * grid_y).

verdicts.reasoning: Read bse_io.py:358-369 signature: positional/keyword args are restart_file, n_val, n_cond, fermi_energy, mesh_xy, pad_bands, then keyword-only input_file, cell_volume, n_occ — no **kwargs catch-all, no use_nohead param anywhere in the 358-480 body. bse_kpm.py:365-372 and bse_pseudopoles.py:592-599 both call it with use_nohead=args.nohead. grep -rn use_nohead src/ tests/ tools/ returns only these two call sites, confirming no definition accepts it anywhere in the repo — every invocation of either CLI (with or without --nohead) raises TypeError at the load call. Also confirmed bse_jax.py:536 (`bse_kpm.main(kpm_argv)`) is the --kpm-dos delegation route, so bse_jax --kpm-dos inherits the crash too. FEAST's --windows-kpm path calls run_kpm_dos directly on already-loaded data (bypasses this loader) so it is correctly noted as unaffected.

grep -rn nohead src/ tests/ tools/ shows --nohead is parsed at bse_kpm.py:355-356 and bse_pseudopoles.py:570-571, and its only consumer is the crashing use_nohead=args.nohead kwarg (see claim 1). grep -n V_qmunu bse_io.py shows only f['V_qmunu'] access, no *_nohead dataset name anywhere in the loader body (358-910). Confirmed apply_q0_head_rank1_sharded (the head-injection machinery the flag was meant to gate) still exists, defined in src/gw/head_correction.py:779 and called from bse_io.py:505 — so this is lost wiring on a still-live feature, not simple dead code. Reclassifying kind label as it reads more like 'bug' (crashes) layered under 'lost wiring', but the reader's own framing already captures this correctly as parsed-but-unread/lost-wiring rather than pure dead code; verdict tag reflects that the flag is functionally broken.

grep -n ring_timing|ring-timing src/bse/bse_jax.py returns only line 481 (the add_argument). Repo-wide grep -rn 'ring_timing|ring-timing' . (excluding .git) returns the same single hit. Checked all mandated call mechanisms: find runs/**/*.sh piped to grep for ring_timing/ring-timing — no hits; grep over skills/**/*.md — no hits; grep over sources/lorrax_D/scripts, tools, docs — no hits. No getattr/string dispatch pattern exists in bse_jax.py's arg-handling (it's a flat if/elif chain on args.<dest>, verified by inspection of the surrounding delegation blocks). Confirmed genuinely dead — the flag is parsed into args.ring_timing but that attribute is never read.

Traced the --lanczos code path: bse_jax.py:609 calls _preview_lanczos(args.input, args.n_val, args.n_cond, n_eig=..., ...) — the call has no px/py argument, and _preview_lanczos's signature (bse_jax.py:203-219) has no px/py parameter at all. Inside _preview_lanczos, the sharded branch builds mesh_xy = create_mesh_2d() with no arguments (line 229), and create_mesh_2d (bse_ring_comm.py:31-43) auto-factorizes len(jax.devices()) when devices=None — ignoring any user px/py entirely. The single-device fallback calls _load_ring_subset(restart_file, n_val, n_cond, 1, 1, ...) with literal 1,1 (line ~295-300). Confirmed args.px/args.py are used only in the ring-check/kpm/feast delegation argv lists (lines 497-498, 520-521, 552-554), never on the lanczos route. Correctly classified as silently-ignored cruft, not a crash.

verdicts.symbol: --eqp/--n-occ silently dropped on bse_jax FEAST and KPM routes
verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed --eqp and --n-occ are defined as global bse_jax args (lines 477-478) with no restriction to --lanczos. grep -n args.eqp|args.n_occ bse_jax.py shows both used only inside the --lanczos branch (lines 617-618). Read the full kpm_argv list (516-536) and feast argv list (543-602) end to end — neither contains --eqp/--n-occ nor any equivalent flag. grep add_argument in bse_feast.py and bse_kpm.py for eqp/n_occ patterns returns nothing — neither sub-CLI defines such a flag, so it cannot even be forwarded. No warning/print exists near either delegation block when args.eqp is set but not forwarded. This produces the described concrete failure: default (FEAST) route with --eqp set silently uses uncorrected DFT eigenvalues with no error or warning.

verdicts.symbol: default kernel is RPA, not BSE
verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed use_rpa = args.rpa or not args.bse at bse_jax.py:515, 542 and include_W=not (args.rpa or not args.bse) at 616 — omitting --bse does silently select RPA. But the --bse help text at line 382-383 literally states 'Use full BSE kernel (D+V-W). Overrides default RPA.' — the RPA default is explicitly documented in-CLI, matching the reader's own classification of 'footgun, not a bug.' Correctly filed as convention.

grep -n 'parse_known_args|parse_args(argv)|parse_args()' src/bse/*.py: bse_jax.py:484 is the only parse_known_args() call among 18 CLI entry points in src/bse; every other file (bse_feast.py, bse_kpm.py, bse_pseudopoles.py, absorption_haydock.py, absorption_eigvecs.py, feast_sweep.py, etc.) uses strict parser.parse_args(argv). Confirmed unique and as described; correctly filed as a convention/footgun rather than a crash-bug.

bse_jax.py:446-449 shows parser.add_argument('--matvec-kind', choices=('ring','gather','simple'), default='ring'). STATUS.md line 16 states 'bse_simple.py | ... | fastest, default --matvec-kind=simple'. Confirmed doc/code mismatch as described; harmless documentation error, correctly classified as convention/doc-drift not a functional bug.

Confirmed absorption_haydock.py:317-318 default=2 for --n-spinor, feeding pref = 16*pi**2/(V_cell*n_k*n_spin*n_spinor) at lines 109/123 — an unconditional 1/n_spinor scaling, so nspinor=2 on a true nspinor=1 (scalar-relativistic) system halves eps2 with no warning. absorption_eigvecs.py by contrast defaults --n-spinor to None and infers it from eigenvectors.h5's spin_kernel attr (line 124), an asymmetric convention between the two CLIs. Checked whether this is an intentional BGW-compat choice: the module docstring (lines 18-20) states 'For our spinor calcs n_spin=1 and n_spinor=2', and STATUS.md:122 confirms the pref formula itself matches BGW absh.f90:46 — so the *formula* is correct/BGW-compatible, but the *default value* of n_spinor is not something haydock can auto-infer (dipole.h5, read via load_dipole_h5 in absorption_common.py, carries no nspinor/spin_kernel attr — only nbands/nk), unlike the eigvecs route. The recommended BGW_COMPARE.md cookbook command (lines 43-53) does not pass --n-spinor explicitly, relying on the default. This is a real, reproducible factor-2 silent error for any non-spinor haydock run, not merely a documented BGW convention — confirmed as bug.

verdicts.symbol: parallel cohsex.in parsers (BSE bypasses gw config layer)
verdicts.reasoning: grep -n 'gw_config|read_lorrax_input' src/bse/*.py returns zero hits — confirmed no cross-import. bse_io.py:584-600 (_parse_wfn_path) and 667-695 (_parse_head_overrides) are independent line-by-line key=value parsers recognizing only wfn_file / vhead / whead_0freq respectively, with no unknown-key warning. gw_config.py:348-349 shows the same vhead/whead_0freq keys already exist in the typed config dict. Genuine single-source-of-truth violation; correctly filed as a refactor target for the BSE cleanup map.

Confirmed two independent implementations: test_bse.py:288-306 defines find_restart_file with candidates = [tmp/isdf_tensors_*.h5, isdf_tensors_*.h5, tmp/taggedarrays600.h5, tmp/taggedarrays.h5, taggedarrays.h5] (5 candidate patterns), while bse_io.py:756-764 (_find_restart_file) only checks [tmp/isdf_tensors_*.h5, isdf_tensors_*.h5] (2 patterns) — these genuinely diverge (test_bse.py has extra taggedarrays.h5 fallbacks bse_io.py lacks), confirming the claim's 'can diverge on glob order/candidates' concern. test_bse.py:337 calls the local find_restart_file, not bse_io's version. Correctly filed as a parallel-path refactor target.

verdicts.symbol: hardcoded machine/dataset defaults in test & sweep CLIs
verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed test_davidson_bse.py:203 default='/pscratch/sd/j/jackm/lorrax_sandbox/runs/Si/04_si_4x4x4_bse/00_bgw_bse_8x8/eigenvectors.h5' — an absolute sandbox path baked into argparse default. Confirmed feast_sweep.py:549-557 defines EXACT_EIGENVALUES_EV for isdf_tensors_600 Si 3x3x1 n_val=4/n_cond=4, and lines 582-589 show it is used by default (exact_eV = EXACT_EIGENVALUES_EV, with a print warning) whenever --full-diag is not passed; feast_zolo_sweep.py:36,305 and feast_ellipse_mixed_sweep.py:31,296 both import and reuse the same hardcoded array. These are dev/debug sweep scripts, not gated tests, so the 'no-16gpu-gating'-style portability rule doesn't directly forbid them, but the reader's characterization (cluster-path + dataset-specific hardcoding baked into defaults) is factually accurate.

verdicts.symbol: two Ry->eV constants in the BSE tree
verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed pseudopoles_eval.py:144 falls back to 13.605693122994 when the H5 attr is absent, while bse_feast.py:29, bse_kpm.py:34, pseudopoles_sweep.py:23 all use 13.6056980659 (RY_TO_EV/RY_TO_EV_DEFAULT). Numeric diff is ~5e-6 eV/Ry, physically negligible; correctly classified as a low-severity consistency smell, not a bug.

Confirmed padding.py:36-64 docstrings explicitly say 'TEST-ONLY' and 'NEVER set this in production runs'. Traced bse_io.py:442 and 877, both call padded_mu_extent(n_rmu, ...), and padded_mu_extent (padding.py:67+) internally calls extra_mu_pad() which reads LORRAX_EXTRA_MU_PAD — confirmed BSE inherits this env knob transitively as described. Correctly classified test-only/informational.

claims.detail: The sandbox docs state the default is 1.0d-12 Ry, but the code default is auto: avgcut = 1/TOL_ZERO (infinity) for untruncated semiconductor screening and TOL_ZERO otherwise — i.e. for 3D semiconductors EVERY fine v(q) in intkernel is a mini-BZ Monte-Carlo average, not the point value 8π/q². A LORRAX fine-grid design that copies the documented default would silently disable this averaging and disagree with BGW at every q.
claims.evidence: inread.f90:120 'xct%avgcut=-1 !FHJ: <0 means "auto"'; inread.f90:901-905: 'if (xct%avgcut<0) then / xct%avgcut = TOL_ZERO / if (xct%icutv==TRUNC_NONE .and. xct%iscreen==SCREEN_SEMICOND) xct%avgcut = 1d0/TOL_ZERO'; docs absorption.inp:282 'the default is 1.0d-12'

claims.detail: fixwings/fixwings_dyn are NOT part of the BSE pipeline — grep finds call sites only in Sigma/sigma_main.f90:1797-1826. BSE wing/head handling lives in mtxel_kernel.calc_wings (coarse-side scaling, mtxel_kernel.f90:1016-1059) plus the bsemat_fac/oneoverq re-add in intkernel.f90:1103-1146. Design docs (and this audit's own prompt) should not cite fixwings as the BSE wing mechanism; it is the Sigma-side mirror of the same convention.
claims.evidence: grep -rn 'call fixwings' BerkeleyGW → only Sigma/sigma_main.f90:1797,1801,1804,1808,1815,1819,1822,1826; grep 'fixwings' BSE/*.f90 → no hits

claims.detail: fact = 16π/celvol is declared (line 123) and assigned (line 398) but never used anywhere in mtxel_kernel.f90 — legacy remnant; the physical prefactor is applied later as factor = −8π/(celvol·nktotal) in intkernel.f90:224. Informational only (BGW is read-only); relevant so designers don't hunt for a phantom 16π factor in the stored bsemat.
claims.evidence: grep -n 'fact' mtxel_kernel.f90 → 123 (decl), 398 (assign), 518/522/524/525 (comment text only); no multiplication site

claims.detail: For velocity-operator runs (qflag==1) BGW reuses the unshifted grid's closest-coarse-point search for the shifted valence grid instead of calling the interpolation twice — an acknowledged approximation ('insignificant difference for typical small q's'). And for qflag==0, delaunay interpolation of the shifted grid falls back to the legacy greedy scheme ('TODO: Fix This!!!', intwfn.f90:398-402). Relevant when validating a future LORRAX velocity-operator fine-grid path against BGW to sub-meV.
claims.evidence: intwfn.f90:376-378 'In principle, we should be calling the below function twice. Once for ik_fi and once for ikq_fi… insignificant difference for typical small q`s'; intwfn.f90:398-402 '! TODO: Fix This!!! Using old interpolation scheme instead of delaunay'

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against sources/BerkeleyGW/BSE/inread.f90: xct%avgcut is initialized to -1 (line 114/120, 'auto') and resolved at lines 899-905: 'if (xct%avgcut<0) then; xct%avgcut = TOL_ZERO; if (xct%icutv==TRUNC_NONE .and. xct%iscreen==SCREEN_SEMICOND) xct%avgcut = 1d0/TOL_ZERO; endif'. Confirmed defaults xct%icutv=TRUNC_NONE (line 102) and xct%iscreen=SCREEN_SEMICOND (line 103) — i.e. the untruncated-semiconductor branch is the out-of-the-box default, so avgcut defaults to 1/TOL_ZERO (huge), not TOL_ZERO. docs/docs_bgw/absorption.inp:282 literally says 'the default is 1.0d-12' with no mention of the auto/TRUNC_NONE+SCREEN_SEMICOND branch. The reader's claim is accurate: the doc is materially incomplete/misleading for the common untruncated-3D-semiconductor case, and a LORRAX design that took the doc's default at face value would silently disable the mini-BZ MC averaging BGW actually performs by default.

grep -rn 'fixwings' BerkeleyGW (all .f90) shows the module fixwings_m is used only via 'use fixwings_m' in Sigma/sigma_main.f90, with call sites at lines 1797,1801,1804,1808,1815,1819,1822,1826 (all in Sigma/sigma_main.f90). No hits in any BSE/*.f90 file. Independently confirmed BSE/mtxel_kernel.f90 has subroutine calc_wings at lines 1016-1059 implementing the analogous wing-scaling logic (SCREEN_SEMICOND / TRUNC_NONE branches, q=0 zeroing), and BSE/intkernel.f90 lines ~1103-1146 apply bsemat_fac with an 'oneoverq' factor for imatrix 1/2 under TRUNC_NONE+SCREEN_SEMICOND — matching the claim's cited BSE-side mechanism. The reader's correction (fixwings is a Sigma-only routine, not part of the BSE fine-grid pipeline) is accurate and directly useful for scoping a design doc correctly.

grep -n '\bfact\b' BSE/mtxel_kernel.f90 returns exactly two lines: 123 (declaration 'real(DP) :: fact' in the main subroutine's local variable block, not module scope) and 398 (sole assignment 'fact = 16.0d0 * PI_D / crys%celvol' inside the ivsaved block). No other read or write site exists in the file; it is never passed as an argument or used in any expression. This is a genuinely unused local variable — informational-only since BGW is read-only per project rules, and correctly distinguished from the real prefactor 'factor = -8.d0*PI_D/(crys%celvol*xct%nktotal)*xct%scaling' declared and used in intkernel.f90:224 (fac_d, fac_x, etc. derive from that, confirmed at intkernel.f90:225-227ish). Claim is accurate.

Confirmed in BSE/intwfn.f90: comment at lines 376-377 (not 376-378, off by one but same text) states the approximation verbatim: 'In principle, we should be calling the below function twice... for velocity operator, it makes insignificant difference for typical small q's.' The code structurally matches: only xct%qflag==2 (explicit finite-Q) and xct%qflag==0 branches call interp_eval/intpts_local again for the shifted grid (kgq_fi); for the default xct%qflag==1 case, no second interpolation call exists, and later at line 457-458 'if (xct%qflag .eq. 1) then; ik_co_q=ik_co' explicitly reuses the unshifted coarse-point index — this is the direct proof of the claim's mechanism. The delaunay qflag==0 TODO comment is confirmed at line 399 ('TODO: Fix This!!! Using old interpolation scheme instead of delaunay', claim said 398-402, actual is 394-405 span, immaterial line-number drift). Both cited approximations are real, correctly described, and relevant for sub-meV LORRAX-vs-BGW validation of a velocity-operator fine-grid path.
